<!-- Topic 4: new and delete -->
<!-- Total slides: 9 -->

# new and delete

---

## How do you ask for memory that outlasts the function? {.smaller}

+ `new` reserves heap memory and returns a pointer to it.
+ `delete` releases that memory back to the system — you are responsible for calling it.

::: notes
Total slides: 9
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## new — allocating a single value

```{.cpp}
int* p = new int;       // allocate — uninitialized
int* q = new int(42);   // allocate and initialize to 42

cout << *q;             // 42
```

- `new` returns a pointer to the allocated memory
- The pointer itself is a local variable — the *memory it points to* is on the heap
- Type determines how many bytes are reserved

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## delete — releasing a single value

```{.cpp}
int* p = new int(42);

// ... use p ...

delete p;       // release the heap memory
p = nullptr;    // prevent accidental reuse
```

- `delete` frees the heap block the pointer points to
- The pointer variable still exists after `delete` — set it to `nullptr` immediately
- Calling `delete` on the same pointer twice is undefined behavior

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Always check the allocation

```{.cpp}
int* p = new (nothrow) int(42);

if (p == nullptr) {
    cerr << "Allocation failed\n";
    return 1;
}

cout << *p << "\n";
delete p;
p = nullptr;
```

::: notes
nothrow version returns nullptr instead of throwing std::bad_alloc. Good habit for systems code. In most course programs, allocation rarely fails — but the pattern is worth showing.
:::

<!-- Slide 4 -->

---

## The complete pattern

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    int* score = new int(0);

    cout << "Enter score: ";
    cin >> *score;

    cout << "You entered: " << *score << "\n";

    delete score;
    score = nullptr;

    return 0;
}
```

::: notes
Walk through each step: allocate, use through dereference, delete, null the pointer. This is the skeleton every heap program follows.
:::

<!-- Slide 5 -->

---

## {background-image="assets/new_single.png" background-size="contain" background-color="#ffffff"}

<!-- Slide 6 -->

---

## Example — heap-allocating a double {.smaller}

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    double* temp = new double(98.6);

    cout << "Temperature: " << *temp << " F\n";

    *temp = 101.3;
    cout << "Fever temp:  " << *temp << " F\n";

    delete temp;
    temp = nullptr;

    return 0;
}
```

::: notes
Simple second example: allocate a double, read through it, modify through it, then clean up. Reinforces that new/delete works the same way for any type — not just int.
:::

<!-- Slide 7 -->

---

## Example — allocating in a function, returning to main {.smaller}

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

double* readTemperature();

int main() {
    double* t = readTemperature();
    cout << "You entered: " << *t << "\n";
    delete t;
    t = nullptr;
    return 0;
}

double* readTemperature() {
    double* p = new double;
    cout << "Enter temperature: ";
    cin >> *p;
    return p;   // caller owns the heap block
}
```

::: notes
Key teaching point: the heap block survives the function return — unlike a local variable. main() receives the pointer and is responsible for delete. This is a common pattern (factory functions) and also a common source of leaks if the caller forgets to delete.
:::

<!-- Slide 8 -->

---

## Summary

+ `new` allocates a heap block and returns a pointer; `delete` releases it — always set the pointer to `nullptr` immediately after.
+ Heap memory outlives the function that created it — the caller is responsible for cleanup.

<!-- Slide 9 -->
